text: Beneath Anbennar's Serpentspine mountains lies the Dwarovar — a vast stacked cave network, home to the dwarven holds. CivStudio surfaces this as a separate Underworld plane you can toggle on the map. Flip it and the sunlit surface world dims to a faint ghost while the cave provinces relight in their true geographic position underground, drawn as warm, amber-rimmed rock floors. Over two hundred caverns are lit this way, sitting exactly where the real Serpentspine cave network runs. This isn't just a cosmetic overlay — settlements founded in these caverns actually live by different rules than surface colonies: no sun, different food, different labor rhythms. The camera doesn't auto-jump when you toggle; you stay where you are and simply see what's really beneath your feet.

title: The Underworld (Serpentspine)
text: Surface settlements track real sunrise and sunset, and a household's daily output scales with how much daylight it gets — eight hours of daylight is treated as a normal, full-output day. Underground there is no sun to track at all, so cavern settlements instead run on a fixed fourteen-hour working day, every day of the year, no seasons, no dawn or dusk — dwarves toiling long lamplit shifts far from any sky. Because that fixed day is nearly twice the eight-hour surface reference, underground labor runs noticeably hotter than a typical surface day, year-round. It's deliberate flavor — the underground trades away weather and sunlight for grueling, unbroken productivity — though it's a powerful economic edge that cave settlements' poor food supply is meant to keep in check.

title: The Underworld (Serpentspine)
text: Every plot in an underground settlement sits on bare cave floor rather than any surface terrain, and its people eat cave fungus instead of crops. That cave floor is food-poor — noticeably worse than open grassland — but rich in raw production, reflecting the ore and stone worked out of the rock. This scarcity is the underground's built-in balancing weight: cavern settlements get a strong labor boost from their unbroken lamplit workday, but a caver colony can't simply out-farm the surface too — its people have to make do with a much thinner food margin than a sunlit field would give them.

title: Special Surface Terrains
text: Beyond the underground itself, several of Anbennar's most storied surface landscapes have been given their own true terrain identity rather than flattening into generic grassland or forest. Deep old-growth ancient forests, the verdant fey gladeways of the elves, the eerie teal fey gladeway variant, blood-magic-soaked crimson bloodgroves, violet fungal mushroom forests (found around Haless), shadowed marshy swamps, and pale glacial ice each render in their own authored color and carry their own food/production/trade yields — forests, swamp, and gladeways also come dressed in their signature tree or wetland cover across most of their land. These aren't underground at all; they're fully sunlit surface terrain, just distinctive enough in Anbennar's lore to deserve their own look and character on the map instead of blending into ordinary land.

title: Names & Dynasties
text: Every soul in CivStudio carries a given name and a dynasty surname drawn to match their race and culture. Humans draw from a vast trove of real-world given names and surnames — tens of thousands of possibilities, so two households rarely share a bloodline by coincidence. Every other people of Anbennar — dwarves, elves, orcs, and the rest — draw their names from that culture's own naming traditions as recorded in the lore of Anbennar itself, so a dwarven clan's names actually sound dwarven, and an elven house's names sound elven, each rooted in its people's real cultural naming conventions rather than a generic fantasy name generator.

title: Names & Dynasties
text: Not all names in CivStudio are equally likely to appear. Within each people's naming tradition, some given names and surnames are common — you'll meet many households bearing them — while others are rare, marking a family as distinctive or exotic even among its own kind. This mirrors how real naming traditions work: a handful of names dominate, while a long tail of unusual ones appears only occasionally. So a rare dynasty surname is a small flavor detail worth noticing — it's a name your colony won't see repeated often.

title: Race (Ancestry)
text: Every person in a colony has a race — an ancestry that shapes who they are as an individual, distinct from their social class or rank. Today two races walk the world: Humans, the default across nearly every settlement, and the Harimari, tiger-folk of Anbennar known for sharp features, clan pride, and a life lived faster than a human's. A single settlement can be mono-racial or a genuine mix, with residents of different ancestries living, working, marrying, and dying side by side. Race governs a person's lifespan and mortality schedule, how quickly they mature into adulthood, and which pool of given names and family surnames they're drawn from — but it never determines their station in life; a Harimari can rise to nobility or languish as a laborer exactly as a human can.

title: Race (Ancestry)
text: A person's race sets the rhythm of their life. Harimari mature remarkably fast: a founding Harimari settler can be as young as 9 years old, and a Harimari recruited off the road as a young adult is typically 9 to 16 years of age. Humans, by contrast, need at least 15 years before founding a household, and a human recruit is usually 16 to 25. Each race also ages and dies on its own mortality schedule, meaning life expectancy and the shape of old age can differ by ancestry. These differences are baked into demography quietly — a mono-racial human colony behaves exactly as it always has, while a mixed-race settlement will visibly show peasants, immigrants, and founders of different races growing up, marrying, and passing on at different paces.

title: Race (Ancestry)
section: The founding race sets colony-wide traditions
text: While each resident's ancestry is personal, a handful of traditions are shared by an entire settlement rather than by each person — there is one calendar of rest days and feasts for the whole colony, and one path of research it pursues. These colony-wide traditions follow the founding ruler's race. A colony founded by a Harimari ruler keeps a Harimari calendar of festivals and leans its research toward the technologies its people favor, even though laborers, immigrants, and spouses of other races living there still keep their own personal ancestry, names, and lifespans. A colony's population mix is set at founding as a blend of races (for example, mostly Human with a Harimari minority); every peasant born into the pool, and every immigrant who arrives, has their ancestry drawn against that blend, while children and spouses always inherit their own family line's race rather than being redrawn.

title: The Harimari
section: A living race with its own culture
text: The Harimari are Anbennar's tiger-folk, and the first race to be given a distinct cultural identity in-game. They carry their own pool of given names — distinctive South Asian and Sanskrit-flavored names for both men and women — and a dynasty book of surnames blending real-world family names with evocative Anbennar clan epithets like 'of the White Stripe' or 'of the Jade Claw,' reserved as the rarest, most prestigious tier a noble founding a new house can draw. A Harimari-led colony keeps its own calendar of feasts — festivals of the hunt, the stripe, and the ancestors, distinct from human holy days — and leans its research toward trade, scholarship, and food production, while gaining little benefit from naval technologies. A noble who founds a fresh dynasty (rather than rising by ennoblement) is the one most likely to plant a rare clan name at the root of a new house.

title: Race (Ancestry)
text: The tree of technologies a colony can research is the same regardless of who founded it — no race is locked out of any discovery. What differs by race is how much a given technology actually helps once researched: a colony's ancestry can make certain techs deliver a strong boost, others a weaker one, and some essentially nothing at all — present in the tree, but quietly inert for that people. A Harimari-led colony, for instance, gains little from naval technologies but leans its bonuses toward trade, scholarship, and food-related discoveries instead. This means two colonies built along the same research path can still feel meaningfully different in practice, purely because of the ancestry that founded them.

title: Settlements: Village and City
text: Every settlement is founded as one of two kinds, decided entirely by the land it's built on. A Village occupies an ordinary province: a single town-center plot and no room to expand beyond it. A City is founded on one of Anbennar's true city provinces, which carry several urban plots - its center plus real districts spreading across them. Which kind a settlement becomes isn't a matter of growth or achievement; it's fixed the moment it's founded, by how much urban land that spot on the map has. A City is meant to be a permanent settlement that endures depopulation, but that protection isn't active yet in practice - today a City can still collapse and empty out exactly the way a Village can, if its households and treasury fail it. Every inhabitable province, Village-sized or City-sized, always has at least one urban plot, so every settlement you found has a town center to build around.

title: Settlements: Village and City
section: Where the town center is founded
text: When a settlement is founded, the exact plot chosen for its town center - and, for a City, its district plots - isn't picked at random or from the middle of the province. Siting favors fresh water above all else: a candidate plot scores highest for touching the coast or lying near a river, with other terrain bonuses like favorable yields or hills counted only as secondary factors. This water-first logic is the same for a humble Village's single town-center plot and for a full City's spread of urban district plots, so a great city's heart is chosen by the same waterside instinct as a lone village's. If a province has no water at all to break the tie, placement simply falls back to the most central plot - so dry, waterless provinces settle exactly where you'd expect.

title: The Rank Ladder
text: Every household in a colony sits on a rung of the rank ladder, and its rung names what it commands rather than what kind of household it is. A common household commands only itself and its family. A noble household commands an estate of firms and property. A ruler commands an entire settlement. Climbing the ladder is a real transformation: the household's people and money carry across, but it becomes a different kind of actor with different powers, banking at a higher tier of currency to match its new standing. Falling down the ladder works the same way in reverse. This ladder is deliberately separate from a family's social class or wealth — a household can be prosperous or poor while still occupying the same rung, since rank tracks scope of command, not standard of living.

title: Ennoblement
text: A colony continually looks among its laborer households for the ablest candidate for the nobility — judged by the head's social standing and practical skill, with youth breaking ties — and lifts that household up. Ennoblement is not a gift of a new family; the same household, the same people, the same accumulated wealth simply moves up a rung. Its money is carried over in full and re-banked from the common copper accounts into the silver accounts nobles use. Overnight, a family that farmed or labored finds itself among the landed and propertied, expected to hold firms, sit on estates, and answer to the throne for taxes owed. The transition takes effect only after the day's ordinary business has settled, so no ongoing trades or wages are disrupted by a household changing rank mid-day.

title: Ruined Nobles
text: Nobility is not permanently secure. A noble household that spends a long stretch of time — roughly a year — sinking deeper into debt rather than climbing out of it is judged ruined and stripped of its rank. Before the fall, its firms and other holdings are handed off to whichever remaining noble in the colony is carrying the lightest load, so nothing is left masterless; if it was the settlement's only noble, those holdings sit unclaimed until the crown next fills a vacant noble seat. The ruined household itself is not destroyed — its people and whatever balance remains are carried down into an ordinary laborer household, re-banked from silver back into copper. In practice most nobles stay solvent by working the settlement's main export trade, so this collapse is the exception rather than the rule.

title: The Crown's Treasury and Bank Taxation
text: Every ruler-led settlement runs three tiers of bank: a copper bank serving the common laborers, a silver bank serving the nobility, and a gold bank that belongs to the crown alone. The ruler taxes the profits the public copper and silver banks earn from their spreads and currency exchange — that tax is real revenue flowing into royal coffers. The crown's own gold bank, however, is never taxed: its retained profit simply IS the treasury. Since the ruler already owns that bank outright, taxing it would just be the crown skimming money from itself into itself, so that double-dip is deliberately excluded from the tax base. In effect, the gold bank's growing equity and the ruler's war-chest are one and the same pool of wealth.

title: Trade Goods
text:
Every province in the world produces a signature trade good — grain, iron, silk, and dozens of others drawn straight from Anbennar's own geography. Unlike the scattered resource bonuses you'll spot on individual patches of land, a trade good belongs to the whole province: it's a defining trait of the place itself, alongside who rules it, what culture its people share, and what faith they follow. An unsettled, undiscovered province shows no trade good at all — its riches are still unknown.

Each good falls into one of five broad classes: Food, Luxury, Strategic, Manufactured, or Magical. The Magical class is distinctly Anbennar — things like damestear, mithril, precursor relics, fungi, and serpentbloom, resources tied to the world's stranger corners rather than ordinary commerce. Right now trade goods are a geographic fact you can observe on the map rather than something your settlements' markets buy, sell, or build around.

title: Trade Goods
text:
Zoom out to the world view and you'll see a small icon resting at the heart of each province — the true regional emblem for whatever that land produces, whether it's a sheaf of grain, an ore vein, or something stranger. These icons only appear when a province is large enough on your screen to read clearly, so the map doesn't clutter as you zoom past. Hover over a province and its trade good's name joins the rest of the tooltip alongside owner and terrain details.

As you zoom in further, toward the level where individual plots and their scattered resource bonuses become visible, the province-wide trade good icon fades out and gives way to the finer per-plot icons — a clean handoff from the big-picture regional resource to the small-scale bounty of each patch of land.

title: The Political Map
text: Beyond the physical map of terrain and rivers, the world can also be viewed in Political mode, which colors every land province by the nation that ruled it at the dawn of history — the opening moment of the Anbennar world, in the year 1444. Each province remembers who owned it, who controlled it, what culture its people spoke, and what faith they followed at that founding moment, drawn from the real, canonical history of Anbennar. Provinces that were unclaimed wilderness, uncolonized frontier, or open sea simply show no political color. This is a snapshot of the world's starting political order — the stage upon which your settlements' own history then unfolds.

title: The Political Map
text: Political mode isn't a single view — it's three, switched with an overlay control alongside the map. Nation mode colors each province by its ruling realm. Culture mode colors by the people's ethnic and linguistic heritage. Faith mode colors by dominant religion. Only one overlay shows at a time, and you can jump straight to any of them via a link. Hovering a province reveals the name and color of whichever entity is currently active, while a detail panel always lists all three at once — nation, culture, and faith together — so you can study a place's full identity even while only one layer is painted on the map itself.

title: The Political Map
text: The Political overlay is designed to get out of the way as you zoom in. From a world overview, provinces are painted in solid, full-strength color by whichever nation, culture, or faith owns them — ideal for surveying realms at a glance. As you zoom closer and the real per-plot terrain, forests, and rivers start rendering beneath, the political fill gradually fades toward transparency, letting the physical landscape show through. Zoom in further still, to individual-province detail, and the fill disappears almost entirely, leaving only colored borders and a highlight on whichever province your cursor rests over — so the terrain, not the paint, dominates close-up.

title: The Political Map
text: Political mode comes with a legend listing every nation, culture, or faith currently on screen, ranked by how much territory each one covers — the biggest players in the current view rise to the top. Hovering an entry in the legend spotlights its provinces on the map. A search box lets you look up any nation, culture, or religion by name directly; picking a result jumps the camera to that entity's largest territory, making it easy to find a realm or people even if you don't know where on the map they sit.

title: The Privy Council
text: Every settlement's leader keeps a Privy Council: a set of advisors, each one a real, named member of the ruling court, who between them let you view the world in different ways. Instead of digging through separate menus, you pick an advisor from a row of portrait buttons along the top of the screen, and the whole view changes to match their expertise — the map recolors, transforms, or gives way entirely to a different display. Only a handful of advisors are active today (Main Map, Foreign, Religion, Technology, Globe View, and Zeitgeist); a few more Civ-style roles are shown as greyed-out placeholders for later. Each advisor has its own hotkey, and a second control strip beneath the main bar changes to offer that advisor's own options.

title: The Privy Council
text: Each Privy Council advisor governs one way of looking at your settlement. The Main Map is the default physical view. The Foreign Advisor recolors the map by nation, with a culture view folded into the same tab. The Religion Advisor recolors it by faith. The Technology Advisor replaces the map entirely with the tech tree, its eras and zoom controls in the strip below. Globe View lets you flip between the surface world and the underworld, and adopts an overhead god's-eye perspective. Zeitgeist is the live, social advisor — it shows the settlement's real-time event feed and carries your sign-in and playback controls. Switching advisors doesn't just change color; it can swap what fills the screen outright, while zoom controls always stay available no matter which advisor you're viewing through.

title: The Privy Council
text: Your advisors are not abstract icons — each one is an actual living noble drawn from the leader's own court, the aristocracy and the ruling household, never the common laboring folk. Their name is shown as the advisor's name, and the portrait reflects their race, drawn from real Anbennar court art. Because every person in the world is mortal, advisors can and do die of old age or misfortune. When that happens, the council doesn't stay empty — the most suitable qualifying noble automatically steps up to fill the vacated role (for instance, the court's most learned mind naturally gravitates to the Technology seat). You'll see a brief notice when a succession happens, and the portrait and name in that advisor's seat update to reflect the new officeholder.

title: The Privy Council
text: Curious about the person actually holding an advisor's seat? Clicking their portrait opens a full character sheet in the side panel: their name, race, and age; their skills shown as bars, with a flame icon marking any passions, and the particular skill that earned them the role highlighted. Below that sits their household — a list of family members, each with their name, relation, and age, and each one clickable to jump to that person's own sheet in turn, with a way back. It's a window into the living, aging, marrying, and dying cast of people actually running your settlement, not just a set of static titles.

title: The Privy Council
text: Zeitgeist is the Privy Council's live and social advisor. While your settlement's event feed is actively streaming, a pulsing red LIVE badge appears — an unmistakable, Twitch-style signal that you're watching history unfold in real time rather than a static snapshot. This is also where your account controls live: signing in, your player handle, signing out, and the playback transport that lets you pause, resume, or adjust the speed of a running session. Play and speed controls only work once you're signed in, which is why the Privy Council groups live-viewing and your account together under the same advisor rather than scattering them separately.

title: The Zoom Spine
text: Zooming the world map isn't just about enlarging pixels — it carries you through three distinct ways of playing. Pulled all the way back, you're in Atlas mode: grand strategy, where nations, cultures, faiths, and whole provinces are what matter, much like studying an old parchment map. Zoom to the middle range and you enter Overland mode, the caravan and travel game — routes, colonies, and physical terrain take center stage. Zoom in deep and you reach Ground mode, a city-builder's view of individual plots, buildings, and the households living on them. The shift between these feels is seamless: the map dissolves smoothly from one regime into the next, with no jarring snap between "levels." What you can click on and interact with changes as you cross each threshold, so the game itself feels different at each depth.

title: The Nine Bands
text: As you zoom from the widest view to the closest, the map passes through nine named rungs. In the grand-strategy range: World (the whole continent), Realm (super-regions), and Region. In the caravan/travel range: Province (borders, names, sea routes appear), Terrain (real ground textures and trade-good icons become visible), and Locale (deep terrain detail, points of interest). In the city-builder range: Plot (per-tile resources and bonuses), Settlement (a colony's buildings and bounds), and Building (the deepest possible zoom, where individual structures and people are legible). Each rung reveals more — province borders and names appear around the Province band, real terrain art and resource icons appear around Terrain, and building footprints only resolve once you're deep in Plot and beyond.

title: Reading Where You Are
text: A small readout near the map's top corner tells you two things at once: how deep you're zoomed, and what you're actually looking at. It might read "Terrain · Sea Tropical" while you're panning open ocean, or "Settlement · Dhenijansar · Smallholding" once you're hovering a living colony. The rung name (World, Terrain, Settlement, and so on) tells you how far you've zoomed; the phrase after it names the actual place, terrain, or settlement under your view's center — always keyed to the crosshair at the middle of the screen, not just whatever happens to be biggest on screen, so it stays stable as you pan and zoom. If nothing notable is there, it says so plainly — "Uncharted ocean," "Open water," "No settlement here."

title: Crossing Between Regimes
text: Because clicking the map means something different in each of the three zoom regimes — picking a nation in Atlas, a caravan or colony in Overland, a plot or building in Ground — the game makes sure you notice when you cross from one into another. The mode readout swaps its icon and label, the cursor itself changes shape (an open hand for Atlas, a route reticle for Overland, a plot crosshair for Ground), and a brief colored flash pulses at the edge of the screen the instant you cross a boundary. These cues fire only on an actual crossing, not on every scroll tick, and the boundaries have a little give built in — so hovering right at a threshold doesn't make the mode flicker back and forth.

title: The City-Micro View
text: Zoom in far enough on a developed settlement and the map stops being an abstract colored blob and starts resolving into individual building lots — rectangles laid out across the urban core, sized and packed more densely wherever the underlying land is more developed. It's an early skeleton of a true city view: at this writing it shows footprints only, not finished building art, streets, or animated townsfolk, but it's enough to make a bustling settlement look like a real settlement rather than a single icon once you get close enough. Deeper still, at the very bottom of the zoom range, individual buildings and (eventually) the people living in them become the things you can select and inspect.

title: How a Day Unfolds
text: Every day in a settlement follows the same fixed order. First, the day's sunrise and sunset are calculated for the colony's location and season. Then every living household and firm looks at yesterday's prices and its own income and decides what to buy or sell — these are intentions, not done deals yet. Next, the banks update their interest rates and pay or charge interest. Then the dead are cleared away and replaced — a peasant promoted, an heir installed, a newcomer admitted. Only after all that do the markets actually clear: every posted offer is matched against real supply and demand and money changes hands at a discovered price. Finally the day's events are written to the record, and inflation is recalculated. The key thing to understand: decisions are made against yesterday's world, and only settle today. Nothing is truly bought or sold until the market clears.

title: Laborers: The Working Households
text: Laborers are the backbone of a settlement — households that earn their bread by selling their work at the local firms. Each is built around a head (and sometimes a spouse) with a name, an age, and a set of twelve personal skills. A laborer weighs how much to spend on food versus little luxuries based on how full its larder is: the emptier the pantry, the more of its earnings go to bread rather than baubles. Laborers eat a modest daily ration — enough to live decently, but far less lavish than what nobles or rulers enjoy — and if a household head cannot feed itself, it starves and the line ends there. When a laborer household dies, in a settlement with a peasant reserve a fresh household is promoted up from that reserve to take its place; in a smaller, self-sufficient colony a same-family heir inherits instead, keeping the household's wealth and name alive.

title: Personal Skills and Training
text: Every person in the world carries twelve personal skills — things like construction, farming, intellect, crafting, and more — each ranked from utterly unskilled to true mastery. Skills aren't fixed: they are trained by doing. A laborer who works the fields daily grows more capable at farming; a noble who works the trading house sharpens their business acumen. Skills also fade with disuse once they rise above a basic competent level — mastery erodes without practice, though the fundamentals never vanish. Some people have a passion for a particular skill, learning it faster and letting it decay more slowly (a truly passionate master never forgets their craft at all). A person's overall aptitude — the average of their twelve skills — marks them as ordinary or exceptional, and unusually gifted individuals tend to carry rarer, more distinctive names.

title: How Wages Are Earned
text: When laborers and firms meet in the labor market, pay isn't equal for equal skill — it's equal per head, but the work delivered scales sharply with ability. A worker who has mastered the trade their firm needs delivers vastly more real output than an unskilled newcomer, even though both draw the same wage from the firm's budget. Working also trains exactly the skills that job calls for — a farmhand grows more skilled at farming, an artisan at craft. How much daylight there is matters too: on the long days of summer, workers get more done; on short winter days, less — though this effect eases the further from the equator a settlement lies, so the depths of winter near the poles don't cripple the economy entirely. A worker's daily commute to a distant field also eats into the working day, so land close to the settlement's heart is worked more efficiently than remote plots.

title: How the Markets Set Prices
text: Goods in a settlement — food, little luxuries, tools — are bought and sold in open markets that search for the price at which what people want to buy roughly matches what's on offer, nudging only a little from the previous day's price rather than swinging wildly. When more people want a good than can get it, that unmet demand is quietly tracked — not just how much goes unfulfilled today, but whether that shortfall is worse than the sector's normal baseline. A sustained, above-normal shortage is a real signal of scarcity worth acting on, distinguishing a passing bad day from a lasting trend. This same signal is what tells a settlement's ruler where new workshops and farms are needed most.

title: Nobles: The Rentier Aristocracy
text: Nobles are not born to their station — every noble family in a settlement was raised up from common laborers, chosen for their sharp intellect and elevated to the aristocracy. A noble owns firms and banks rather than laboring at them, drawing a share of their profits and dividends each day and spending a portion back into the local markets, letting that wealth recirculate through the settlement. Nobles keep a well-stocked household larder and eat a genuinely lavish diet, never going hungry — though like anyone, they age and eventually pass their holdings to an heir of the same bloodline, so a noble house, once raised, endures across generations. A noble's intellect also directly powers the settlement's foreign trade — the more capable the noble working it, the more the settlement earns from its exports.

title: Ennoblement: Rising to the Aristocracy
text: No settlement founds its aristocracy from nothing — nobles are made, not born into privilege. In the settlement's early weeks, the ruler steadily elevates the most intellectually gifted laborers into noble households, one every so often, until the aristocracy reaches its intended size — commonly around five great houses. Until that aristocracy is fully staffed, the ruler personally works the export trade to keep it running. If ever a new workshop is chartered and there isn't a single noble left to own it, one more laborer is elevated on the spot to take up the role. Once raised, a noble house passes down through its bloodline rather than being refilled from below — the meritocratic elevation only tops the ranks back up if a house dies out entirely.

title: The Ruler and the Crown Treasury
text: Every proper settlement has a ruler — its sovereign head of state, holding the treasury and answering to no one. The ruler taxes a slice of the public banks' profits and of every noble's income, all flowing into the crown's own coffers, and spends a little on its own indulgent pleasures while dining on the most sumptuous rations in the land. The ruler funds the founding of the settlement's entire workforce out of its treasury, personally works the export trade during the early days before the aristocracy is fully raised, and once a month reviews the whole economy — chartering new workshops where demand is strong, and in principle closing failing ones where it's overbuilt. Like any household, the ruler eventually passes the crown to an heir of the same line, so the treasury and the line of succession endure across generations.

title: Growing the Settlement: The Builder
text: A settlement does not simply exist on however much land it started with — it grows, plot by plot, and the builder is the crew that makes that growth happen. Staffed entirely by peasants drawn from the settlement's common reserve, the builder clears wild, overgrown land and readies it to host a farm, workshop, or other enterprise. Whoever requested the new plot — the firm that needs it — pays for the work; and because the peasants doing the clearing are wards of the ruler, their wages ultimately flow back to the crown that feeds them, with the labor itself sharpening their skill at construction. Without a builder, a living settlement can never expand past however much land it started with; a settlement with one grows to fill its land as its economy demands more farms and workshops, right up to the natural limit the surrounding countryside allows.
